tate of CalifoIrtia

615 South Flower Street, Suite 1201 
Los Angeles, CA 
90017 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-83-234 
Dear Mr. Aleshire: 
You reques~ed advice from this ~ffice on beh~lf of Cecil 
Green, a member of the Norwa~k City Council, concerning his 
duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act. 
My understanding of the situation is as follows. 
Mr. Green 
has a business arrangement with Mr. Neal Irving of Neal Irving 
and Associates, Environmental Consultants. 
I will refer 
hereinafter to Mr. Irving and Neal Irving and Associates 
collectively as "Irving."ll 
According to your letter of 
September 20, 1983, to Mr. Green, Green and Irving "refer 
business to each other and occasionally represent each other in 
exchange for a percentage of the income from those particular 
jobs." 
Green has the title of Associate Executive 
Vice-President in Neal Irving and Associates, but he has no 
management authority or any investment in the company. 
In 
addition, Green does not receive any salary or other benefits 
from the company other than the percentages referred to above. 
An appeal is pending before the Norwalk City Council of a 
Planning Commission denial of a conditional use permit and 
II 
I assume that Mr. Irving is either the sole or 
majority shareholder in Neal Irving and Associates and that, 
therefore, for the purposes of our analysis, they are one 
entity. 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983 
Page 2 
preC1se development plan involving a gasoline service station. 
The appellant, ARCO, is represented by Neal Irving and 
Associates in this proceeding. 
Mr. Irving's fees for this 
matter are not contingent on the result of the appeal but are 
instead based on actual time spent. 
Mr. Green is not involved in the ARCO appeal as part of his 
arrangement with Mr. Irving, and he will not receive any fees 
from the matter. 
ARCO is not a source of income to him nor does 
he have any investment or other finanCial interest in ARCO. 
However, Mr. Green has represented Mr. Irving (but not ARCO) in 
connection with gasoline service stations in other cities. 
When the ARCO appeal first carne before the City Council on 
September 12, 1983, Mr. Green stated that he would abstain from 
participation in the public hearing and abstain from voting. 
The vote on the appeal was a tie vote (2-2), and Mr. Green then 
requested your opinion on whether he could participate or 
whether he had a conflict of interest based on the above recited 
fa~ts. 
You advised him in your letter of September 30~ 1983, 
that a confli6t of ·interest under the Political"Reform Act might 
exist~ Mr. Green then requested that you obtain advice from 
this office. 
You have also indicated to me that you would like 
some general guidance to assist you in ¥our analysis of future 
situationi. 
ANALYSIS 
Government Code Section 87100 2/ prohibits public officials 
from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting 
to use their official positions to influence a governmental 
decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a 
financial interest. 
Financial interest is defined in Section 87103 as a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the decision 
on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
~ All statutory references are to the California 
Government Code. 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983 
page 3 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position, of management. 
1. 
Sources of Income. 
Assuming that Mr. Green has received 
$250 or more from Irving during the last 12 months, Irving IS a 
"source of income" to Mr. Green within the meaning of Section 
87l03(c).11 
Thus Mr. Green may not participate in any Council 
decisions which could have a material financial effect on 
Irving. 
For your future reference, I would like to note who are 
other sources of income to Mr. Green as a result of his 
arrangement with Irving. 
Any persons or business entities that 
he has represented or done other work for are sources of income 
to him if he has received $250 or more for that representation 
or work. 
In addition, for purposes of conflict of interest 
analysis, an official is presumed to be a "source of income" to 
himself, and he may not participate in any governmental 
decisions which could materially affect his own pocket. 
2. 
Effects on Sources of Income. 
The Commission has 
provided guidelines for determining whether a decision will have 
a material financial effect on a source of income to an official 
in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (3) (copy enclosed) : 
(A) 
The effect of the decision will be to 
directly increase or decrease the amount of income 
(other than rents) to be received by the official, or 
to confer a financial benefit or detriment upon the 
11 
In addition, since Mr. Green is an officer of Neal 
Irving and Associates, sUbsection (d) of 87103 is applicable 
also. 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983 
Page 4 
official or a member of the official's immediate 
family, in an amount of one hundred dollars ($100) or 
more; or 
(B) 
There is a nexus between the governmental 
decision and the purpose for which the official 
receives income; or 
(C) 
In the case of a source of income which is' a 
business entity, the business entity will be affected 
in a manner described in subsection (b) (1) above; or 
(D) 
If the source of income is not a business 
entity, the decision will have a significant effect on 
the source. 
Under the facts presented, if the decision could have a 
material financi~l effect on Irving, Mr. Green ~hould continue 
to abstain from participation in the matter. 
Or, if there is a-
nexus between the decision and the purpose for which Mr. Green 
receives income from one of his.clients or from Irving, 
Mr. Green should d{squalify himself. 
I will discuss each of 
these possibilities separately. 
a. 
Effects on Irving. 
As stated above, Irving is not being 
paid by ARCO on a contingent basis. 
In other words, regardless 
of the outcome, Irving will be paid the same amount. 
This would 
suggest that Irving will not be affected by the Council 
decision, and that, therefore, this would not be a basis for 
disqualification by Mr. Green. 
However, there may be other 
financial effects on Irving. 
For example, it is possible that 
the decision could affect Irving's business relationship with 
ARCO in a material way. 
Since we do not have enough facts to 
come to a conclusion on this issue, I suggest that Mr. Green 
consider the matter himself very carefully. 
b. 
Nexus Test. 
The second potential ground for disqualifi-
cation is the nexus test. 
Simply stated, this provides that an 
official may not accomplish in his public capacity what he is 
paid to do in his private capacity. 
The most obvious but least 
common application of the nexus test occurs where an official 
has been hired to promote a specific proposal for h~s employer 
before the governmental body on which he sits. 
However, the 
nexus test is normally triggered where an official is being paid 
to promote or lobby a particular policy or position on behalf of 
an association and that association appears before a govern-
mental entity for which he is a decision maker in connection 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983 
Page 5 
with that policy or position. 
See the staff advice letter to 
Linda Bes t (No. A-81-032) 
(copy enclosed). 
It does not appear from the facts that you have presented 
that there is a nexus between the kind of work Mr. Green 
performs for Mr. Irving and the decision pending before the City 
Council. 
You explained to me that Mr. Green's work with 
Mr. Irving consists of representing clients with particular 
plans in permit or other similar governmental proceedings 
outside of Norwalk. 
Unless one of the clients he has 
represented is involved in this matter, a nexus does not exist. 
The fact that he has represented Irving on matters involving gas 
stations in other jurisdictions does not, by itself, establish a 
nexus. 
In summary, based on the facts presented, Mr. Green does not 
have a conflict of interest in the decision before the City 
Council involving the ARCO appeal. 
Howev~r, we give this advice 
with the caveat that both you and Mr. Green, who are more-
familiar with allot the circumstances, should analyze the 
situation in light of our analysis. 
. 
You also requested our advice concerning the application of 
common law conflict of interest doctrine to this situatio.n •. 
This office only advises on matters concerning the Political 
Reform Act. 
If you would like to discuss this letter or if you have 
further questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-6444.

Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq. 
Chief, Legal Division

P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Re: 
Advice for Norwalk City Councilman Cecil Green 
.. 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
Councilman Cec.il Green of the City of Norwalk has 
requested.this office as City Attorney to obtain a written 
adviso'ry letter from the FC?-ir Political Practices Commission as 
to whether Councilman Green may participate in an appeal hearing 
of the Planning Commission's denial of a conditional use permit. 
The appeal hearing has been continued to November 14 to allow 
the Commission time to provide the advice. 
Enclosed you will find a letter which we wrote to 
Councilman Green which summarizes the facts which have been 
provided to us and our analysis. 
Based upon this letter, 
Councilman Green elected to seek written advice from the 
Commission pursuant to Government Code § 83114. 
In.addition.to any issues which you feel are 
relevant, we would request that the following issues be 
addressed: 
1. 
Presuming Councilman Green receives over $250 
in income from and is a honorary officer of a 
business entity which represents an applicant 
in an appeal being considered by the city 
council, does Councilman Green have a financial 
interest in the City Council's decision? 
2. 
Is there a sufficient "nexus" between the decision 
of the City Council and the purpose for which 
Councilman Green's services are retained in order 
to constitute a material financial effect within 
the meaning of Title 2 California Administrative 
Code § l8702? 


•. 1. K€NN€TH SROWN

TELEPHON E 
(213) 624-1001 
September 30, 1983 
Mr. Cecil Green 
City council of Norwalk 
City of Norwalk 
12700 Norwalk Boulevard 
Norwalk, California 
90650 
Re: 
Vote on Conditional User Permit No. 445, 
and Precise Development Plan No. 522R 
Dear Cecil: 
/ 
OF COUNSEL 
MAX HAt.,F"ON 
On September 12, 1983, the city Council heard the 
appeal of the Planning Commission's denial of Conditional Use 
Permit Number 445 and Precise Development Plan Number 522R. 
At the beginning of that hearing you stated that you would 
abstain from participation in the public hearing and would 
abstain from voting. 
At the conclusion of the hearing, 
however, the vote on a motion to approve the appeal resulted 
i~ a tie vote. 
At that time you requested an opinion of the 
ci ty Attorney as to whether you could participate in the 
matter, or whether you should continue to abstain based upon 
a possible conflict of interest. 
The ... facts -which you hav'e provided this office 
regarding the possible conflict are as follows. 
The Condi-
tional Use Permit and Precise Development Plan approval are 
being sought by ARCO as the lessee of certain property. 
In 
connection with that approval, ARCO has retained Neal Irving 
& 
Associates, 
Environmental 
Consultants, 
(hereinafter 
1/ Irving") to present ARCO I S request to the City. 
You have 
-informed 
us 
that you have 
a 
business relationship with 
Irving, whereby you and Irving refer business to each other 
and occasionally represent each other in exchange for a 
percentage of the income from those particular jobs. 
Occa-
sionally 
you 
may 
represent 
Irving 
at public 
hearings 
involving Target Service stations of a type similar to the 
one being sought in the City of Norwalk. 
Furthermore, you 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983 
Page 2 
have 
been 
given the title of Associate Executive Vice-
President of Irving, although you have indicated that the 
ti tIe is "honorary" and you have no management authority. 
Furthermore, you state that you are not considered to be an 
employee of that company, are not on the payroll, have no 
benefits and have no ownership or other interest in Irving. 
We are advised that you do not represent ARCO or the property 
owner, and that you have no interest, economic or otherwise, 
in either ARCO or the current proposal and will recei ve 
nothing from the approval or disapproval of the project. 
Finally, Irving has no interest in ARCO or in the property 
which is the subject of the appeal hearing, and Irving's fees 
are based solely on the time spent and are not contingent in 
any manner. 
We have concluded that Irving is a source of income 
to you and that you may be an officer or employee of Irving 
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
It further 
appears that, although the decision will not have a financial 
effect on you· directly,. it could be argued that there is a 
"nexus" between this decision and your position with Irving. 
Furthermore, 
there is 
a possibility that a 
conflict of 
interest might be found under the common law doctrine if you 
participate in this decision. 
While there is considerable 
ambiguity in the law on these points, we are unable to say 
that a conflict does not exist. 
We would suggest that any 
defini ti ve opinion must come from the Fair Political Prac-
tices Commission itself. 
There are several different statutes which control 
conflicts of interest, as well as a nonstatutory, common law 
doctrine, which may apply in this situation. 
Government Code 
Section 87100 (all section references hereinafter are to the 
Government Code) 
states: 
"No public official at ·any level of 
State or local government shall make, 
participate in making or in 
any 
way 
attempt to use his official capacity to 
influence 
a 
governmental 
decision 
in 
which he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 1I 
The term "financial interest" is defined by section 
87103, which states, in part, as follows: 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983 
Page 3 
IIAn official has a financial interest in 
decision wi thin the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have a material 
financial 
effect, 
distinguishable 
from 
its effect, generally on: 
lI(a) Any business entity in which 
the public official has 
a 
direct or 
indirect 
investment 
worth 
more 
than 
One Thousand Dollars ($1,000). 
II (b) A real property in which the 
public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth more than One Thousand 
Dollars ($1,000). 
1/ (c) Anv 
source of. income, 
other 
than loans-SY a commercial lending insti-
tution 
within 
th"e 
regular 
course 
of 
busines's on terms available to the public 
without 
regard 
to 
official 
status, 
aggregating 
Two 
Hundred Fifty Dollars 
($250) 
or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public 
official wi thin 12 months prior to the 
time in which the decision is made. 
I/(d) Any business entity in which 
the public official is a director, offi-
~, partner, trustee, employee or hOlds 
any position of management. II 
"For purposes of this section, indirect 
investment or interest means any invest-
ment or interest owned by the spouse or 
dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, 
or by a business entity or trust in which 
the 
official, 
the 
official's 
agent, 
spouse or dependent children own directly 
or 
indirectly or beneficially 
a 
10% 
interest or greater." 
(Emphasis added.) 
To resolve the conflict of interest issue, we must 
ask two questions: 
(1) do you have one of the types of 
interests that are covered by Section 87103, and (2) is it 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983 
Page 4 
reasonably 
foreseeable 
that this 
material financial effect on such 
to the interest covered by Section 
have been given, it is clear that: 
decision 
will 
have 
a 
interests? 
with regards 
87103, from the facts we 
(1) 
You do not have an investment specified 
by Section 82034 worth more than $1,000 in either ARCO or 
Irving, 
(2) 
You do not have any interest worth more 
than $1,000 in real property which will be effected by this 
decision, 
(3) 
ARCO is not a source of income to you 
worth more than $250, although Irving may be~and 
(4) 
You are not a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, 
employee or manager of ARCO, 
but you do have a 
ti tIe of Associates Executive Vice President with Irving, 
although apparently this title do~ot confe~ any authority. 
From the foregoing, it appears that the material 
financial effect of the decision needs only be analyzed with 
regard to its effect on Irving as a source of income to you 
and as a business entity with which you hold a so-called 
"honoraryll title. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission has enacted 
regulations to be used to help determine if a decision will 
have a material financial effect on a source of income or a 
business entity in which the official has a management role. 
In Titl~ 2, California Administrative Code Section 18702, the 
following have,. been held to be material financial effects: 
(1) 
I f 
the effect of the decision would be to 
increase or decrease the income received by the official by 
$100 or more; 
(2 ) 
"decision and 
income; or 
I f there is a nexus between the governmental 
the purpose for which the official receives 
(3) 
If the official's source 
of 
income is 
a 
business entity, if the decision will increase or decrease 
the annualized gross revenue of that entity by the lesser of 
$100,000 or one percent, but not less than $1,000; or if it 
will increase or decrease the annual net income of the entity 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
september 30, 1983 
Page 5 
by the lesser of $50,000 or one half of one percent, but not 
less than $1,000; or if it will increase or decrease the 
current assets or liability of that entity by the lesser of 
$100,000 or one half of one percent, but not less than 
$1,000. 
Based upon the facts which we have been given, 
since you personally will receive no income from the approval 
or disapproval of this decision, Paragraph (1) above does not 
apply. 
Moreover, the criteria in Paragraph (3) would only be 
triggered if the gross revenue, net income or assets of 
Irving were significantly effected by the decision. 
Since 
the fee arrangement is apparently the normal consultant fee 
based upon time and materials, Irving would be compensated on 
the basis of time actually spent and not the outcome of the 
decision. 
. 
The area where we are very troubled with regard to 
Paragraph (2) and the fact that a material financial effect 
can exist is there 
~s a 
"nexus between the governmental 
decision and the purpose for which an official receives 
income." 
There are no controlling precedents on the meaning 
of this language. 
It is not inconceivable to us, however, 
that the Fair Political Practices Commission could find a 
"nexus" where between a decision to approve a CUP and a 
business relationship where a business entity has hired a 
public official to represent certain clients before govern-
mental bodies in cases involving land use decisions. 
One 
could argue that Irving's purpose in entering into a business 
relationship with a councilmember was to develop the contacts 
and influence of the councilmember with other governmental 
agencies. 
Lacking any precise definition of what the Fair 
Political Practices Commission means by the above nexus 
terminology, we are unable to say that a problem does not 
exist. 
We are further troubled by the implications of the 
common law doctrine against conflicts of interest which exist 
.entirely apart from the provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
Again, however, the parameters of the common law doc-
trine against conflicts of interest are extremely vague. 
As stated by the Attorney General in 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
916, 922-923: 
26w09b 
IIPrior 
to 
the 
enactment 
of 
the 
Political Reform Act of 1974, this office 
predicated decisions 
on noncontractual 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983 
Page 6 
conflict of interest questions 
on the 
common law rule against conflicts. 
We 
have assumed the continuing viability of 
the rule as a cumulative test despite the 
1974 initiative measure which covers both 
contractual and noncontractual matters. 
[Citation.] 
such 
doctrine 
... 
strictly requires- public 
officers 
to 
avoid placing themselves in ~ position In 
which personal interest may 
corne into 
conflict with their duty to thilPllblic.' 
[Citations.]" 
(Emphasis added.) 
Perhaps the most important word in that doctrine is 
"may,1/ which is used to indicate that the mere possibility 
that there might be a conflict between the official's duties 
to the public and to his private interests must be avoided. 
In the' situation at hand, the relationship between yourself 
and Irving which is continuous and which acts to both of your 
benefits, 
the fact .you 
dO 
work occasionally on service 
stations of the type which is in question, and the fact that 
you initially abstained from participating until sucp time as 
a tie vote was indicated, suggest that a vote by you on the 
item in question could corne within that common law prohibi-
tion, and be deemed to be malfeasance in office. 
Unfortunately, in the area of conflict of interest, 
the relevant statutes and regulations are in some instances 
vague 
and there are been some conflicting precedents in 
interpreting them. 
Although we normally strive to give clear 
advice and we are not convinced that a conflict does exist, 
in this. instance, there are enough factors involved that we 
are unable to .. say without hesitation that no conflict does 
exist. 
It would appear that the business relationships and 
understandings 
between you 
and Mr. Irving are oral and, 
therefore, possibly, subject to differing interpretations. 
It would be our suggestion, especially if it is possible 
that Mr. Irving will be representing other business clients 
.before the City Council, that the relationship between you 
and Mr. Irving be clarified or that possibly an opinion be 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983 
Page 7 
requested from the Fair Political Practices Commission which 
would deal with this subject. 
I look forward to discussing this matter with you 
further. 
Sincerely, 
DAVID J. ALESHIRE 
SRS:DJA:esa 
cc: 
Mr. Richard Streng 
26w09b 


Ms. Barbara Milman, Esq. 
October 12, 1983 
Page Two 
3. 
If there is no such "nexus," is there any other 
way in which the decision has a material financial 
effect on a financial interest of Councilman Green 
in order to lead to a possible violation of the 
Political Reform Act? 
4. 
Would Councilman Green's participation in the 
determination of the appeal constitute a violation 
of the common law doctrine against conflicts of 
. interest? 
We would appreciate your earliest response to this 
matter so that the information may "be forwarded to the Council 
prior to its continued hearing on the ap'E,'eal.·· Thank you for 
your consideration in this matter. 
SRS/jarm

Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Diane Fishburn, an attorney 
in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission. 
If you have any questions about your advice 
request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 
working days.

sections.question: You requested advice from this office on behalf of Cecil Green, a member of the Norwalk City Council, concerning his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. Mr. Green has a business arrangement with Mr. Neal Irving of Neal Irving and Associates, Environmental Consultants. According to your letter of September 20, 1983, to Mr. Green, Green and Irving "refer business to each other and occasionally represent each other in exchange for a percentage of the income from those particular jobs." Green has the title of Associate Executive Vice-President in Neal Irving and Associates, but he has no management authority or any investment in the company. An appeal is pending before the Norwalk City Council of a Planning Commission denial of a conditional use permit and precise development plan involving a gasoline service station. The appellant, ARCO, is represented by Neal Irving and Associates in this proceeding.
sections.conclusion: In summary, based on the facts presented, Mr. Green does not have a conflict of interest in the decision before the City Council involving the ARCO appeal. However, we give this advice with the caveat that both you and Mr. Green, who are more-familiar with all of the circumstances, should analyze the situation in light of our analysis.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 87100 2/ prohibits public officials 
from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting 
to use their official positions to influence a governmental 
decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a 
financial interest. 
Financial interest is defined in Section 87103 as a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the decision 
on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
~ All statutory references are to the California 
Government Code. 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983  
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position, of management. 
1. 
Sources of Income. 
Assuming that Mr. Green has received 
$250 or more from Irving during the last 12 months, Irving IS a 
"source of income" to Mr. Green within the meaning of Section 
87l03(c).11 
Thus Mr. Green may not participate in any Council 
decisions which could have a material financial effect on 
Irving. 
For your future reference, I would like to note who are 
other sources of income to Mr. Green as a result of his 
arrangement with Irving. 
Any persons or business entities that 
he has represented or done other work for are sources of income 
to him if he has received $250 or more for that representation 
or work. 
In addition, for purposes of conflict of interest 
analysis, an official is presumed to be a "source of income" to 
himself, and he may not participate in any governmental 
decisions which could materially affect his own pocket. 
2. 
Effects on Sources of Income. 
The Commission has 
provided guidelines for determining whether a decision will have 
a material financial effect on a source of income to an official 
in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (3) (copy enclosed) : 
(A) 
The effect of the decision will be to 
directly increase or decrease the amount of income 
(other than rents) to be received by the official, or 
to confer a financial benefit or detriment upon the 
11 
In addition, since Mr. Green is an officer of Neal 
Irving and Associates, sUbsection (d) of 87103 is applicable 
also. 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983  
official or a member of the official's immediate 
family, in an amount of one hundred dollars ($100) or 
more; or 
(B) 
There is a nexus between the governmental 
decision and the purpose for which the official 
receives income; or 
(C) 
In the case of a source of income which is' a 
business entity, the business entity will be affected 
in a manner described in subsection (b) (1) above; or 
(D) 
If the source of income is not a business 
entity, the decision will have a significant effect on 
the source. 
Under the facts presented, if the decision could have a 
material financi~l effect on Irving, Mr. Green ~hould continue 
to abstain from participation in the matter. 
Or, if there is a-
nexus between the decision and the purpose for which Mr. Green 
receives income from one of his.clients or from Irving, 
Mr. Green should d{squalify himself. 
I will discuss each of 
these possibilities separately. 
a. 
Effects on Irving. 
As stated above, Irving is not being 
paid by ARCO on a contingent basis. 
In other words, regardless 
of the outcome, Irving will be paid the same amount. 
This would 
suggest that Irving will not be affected by the Council 
decision, and that, therefore, this would not be a basis for 
disqualification by Mr. Green. 
However, there may be other 
financial effects on Irving. 
For example, it is possible that 
the decision could affect Irving's business relationship with 
ARCO in a material way. 
Since we do not have enough facts to 
come to a conclusion on this issue, I suggest that Mr. Green 
consider the matter himself very carefully. 
b. 
Nexus Test. 
The second potential ground for disqualifi-
cation is the nexus test. 
Simply stated, this provides that an 
official may not accomplish in his public capacity what he is 
paid to do in his private capacity. 
The most obvious but least 
common application of the nexus test occurs where an official 
has been hired to promote a specific proposal for h~s employer 
before the governmental body on which he sits. 
However, the 
nexus test is normally triggered where an official is being paid 
to promote or lobby a particular policy or position on behalf of 
an association and that association appears before a govern-
mental entity for which he is a decision maker in connection 


David J. Aleshire, Esq. 
December 12, 1983  
with that policy or position. 
See the staff advice letter to 
Linda Bes t (No. A-81-032) 
(copy enclosed). 
It does not appear from the facts that you have presented 
that there is a nexus between the kind of work Mr. Green 
performs for Mr. Irving and the decision pending before the City 
Council. 
You explained to me that Mr. Green's work with 
Mr. Irving consists of representing clients with particular 
plans in permit or other similar governmental proceedings 
outside of Norwalk. 
Unless one of the clients he has 
represented is involved in this matter, a nexus does not exist. 
The fact that he has represented Irving on matters involving gas 
stations in other jurisdictions does not, by itself, establish a 
nexus. 
In summary, based on the facts presented, Mr. Green does not 
have a conflict of interest in the decision before the City 
Council involving the ARCO appeal. 
Howev~r, we give this advice 
with the caveat that both you and Mr. Green, who are more-
familiar with allot the circumstances, should analyze the 
situation in light of our analysis. 
. 
You also requested our advice concerning the application of 
common law conflict of interest doctrine to this situatio.n •. 
This office only advises on matters concerning the Political 
Reform Act. 
If you would like to discuss this letter or if you have 
further questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-6444.

Re: 
Advice for Norwalk City Councilman Cecil Green 
.. 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
Councilman Cec.il Green of the City of Norwalk has 
requested.this office as City Attorney to obtain a written 
adviso'ry letter from the FC?-ir Political Practices Commission as 
to whether Councilman Green may participate in an appeal hearing 
of the Planning Commission's denial of a conditional use permit. 
The appeal hearing has been continued to November 14 to allow 
the Commission time to provide the advice. 
Enclosed you will find a letter which we wrote to 
Councilman Green which summarizes the facts which have been 
provided to us and our analysis. 
Based upon this letter, 
Councilman Green elected to seek written advice from the 
Commission pursuant to Government Code § 83114. 
In.addition.to any issues which you feel are 
relevant, we would request that the following issues be 
addressed: 
1. 
Presuming Councilman Green receives over $250 
in income from and is a honorary officer of a 
business entity which represents an applicant 
in an appeal being considered by the city 
council, does Councilman Green have a financial 
interest in the City Council's decision? 
2. 
Is there a sufficient "nexus" between the decision 
of the City Council and the purpose for which 
Councilman Green's services are retained in order 
to constitute a material financial effect within 
the meaning of Title 2 California Administrative 
Code § l8702? 


•. 1. K€NN€TH SROWN 
T'~OMAS ~. WINFIELO 111

Re: 
Vote on Conditional User Permit No. 445, 
and Precise Development Plan No. 522R 
Dear Cecil: 
/ 
OF COUNSEL 
MAX HAt.,F"ON 
On September 12, 1983, the city Council heard the 
appeal of the Planning Commission's denial of Conditional Use 
Permit Number 445 and Precise Development Plan Number 522R. 
At the beginning of that hearing you stated that you would 
abstain from participation in the public hearing and would 
abstain from voting. 
At the conclusion of the hearing, 
however, the vote on a motion to approve the appeal resulted 
i~ a tie vote. 
At that time you requested an opinion of the 
ci ty Attorney as to whether you could participate in the 
matter, or whether you should continue to abstain based upon 
a possible conflict of interest. 
The ... facts -which you hav'e provided this office 
regarding the possible conflict are as follows. 
The Condi-
tional Use Permit and Precise Development Plan approval are 
being sought by ARCO as the lessee of certain property. 
In 
connection with that approval, ARCO has retained Neal Irving 
& 
Associates, 
Environmental 
Consultants, 
(hereinafter 
1/ Irving") to present ARCO I S request to the City. 
You have 
-informed 
us 
that you have 
a 
business relationship with 
Irving, whereby you and Irving refer business to each other 
and occasionally represent each other in exchange for a 
percentage of the income from those particular jobs. 
Occa-
sionally 
you 
may 
represent 
Irving 
at public 
hearings 
involving Target Service stations of a type similar to the 
one being sought in the City of Norwalk. 
Furthermore, you 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983  
have 
been 
given the title of Associate Executive Vice-
President of Irving, although you have indicated that the 
ti tIe is "honorary" and you have no management authority. 
Furthermore, you state that you are not considered to be an 
employee of that company, are not on the payroll, have no 
benefits and have no ownership or other interest in Irving. 
We are advised that you do not represent ARCO or the property 
owner, and that you have no interest, economic or otherwise, 
in either ARCO or the current proposal and will recei ve 
nothing from the approval or disapproval of the project. 
Finally, Irving has no interest in ARCO or in the property 
which is the subject of the appeal hearing, and Irving's fees 
are based solely on the time spent and are not contingent in 
any manner. 
We have concluded that Irving is a source of income 
to you and that you may be an officer or employee of Irving 
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. 
It further 
appears that, although the decision will not have a financial 
effect on you· directly,. it could be argued that there is a 
"nexus" between this decision and your position with Irving. 
Furthermore, 
there is 
a possibility that a 
conflict of 
interest might be found under the common law doctrine if you 
participate in this decision. 
While there is considerable 
ambiguity in the law on these points, we are unable to say 
that a conflict does not exist. 
We would suggest that any 
defini ti ve opinion must come from the Fair Political Prac-
tices Commission itself. 
There are several different statutes which control 
conflicts of interest, as well as a nonstatutory, common law 
doctrine, which may apply in this situation. 
Government Code 
Section 87100 (all section references hereinafter are to the 
Government Code) 
states: 
"No public official at ·any level of 
State or local government shall make, 
participate in making or in 
any 
way 
attempt to use his official capacity to 
influence 
a 
governmental 
decision 
in 
which he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 1I 
The term "financial interest" is defined by section 
87103, which states, in part, as follows: 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983  
IIAn official has a financial interest in 
decision wi thin the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decision will have a material 
financial 
effect, 
distinguishable 
from 
its effect, generally on: 
lI(a) Any business entity in which 
the public official has 
a 
direct or 
indirect 
investment 
worth 
more 
than 
One Thousand Dollars ($1,000). 
II (b) A real property in which the 
public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth more than One Thousand 
Dollars ($1,000). 
1/ (c) Anv 
source of. income, 
other 
than loans-SY a commercial lending insti-
tution 
within 
th"e 
regular 
course 
of 
busines's on terms available to the public 
without 
regard 
to 
official 
status, 
aggregating 
Two 
Hundred Fifty Dollars 
($250) 
or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public 
official wi thin 12 months prior to the 
time in which the decision is made. 
I/(d) Any business entity in which 
the public official is a director, offi-
~, partner, trustee, employee or hOlds 
any position of management. II 
"For purposes of this section, indirect 
investment or interest means any invest-
ment or interest owned by the spouse or 
dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, 
or by a business entity or trust in which 
the 
official, 
the 
official's 
agent, 
spouse or dependent children own directly 
or 
indirectly or beneficially 
a 
10% 
interest or greater." 
(Emphasis added.) 
To resolve the conflict of interest issue, we must 
ask two questions: 
(1) do you have one of the types of 
interests that are covered by Section 87103, and (2) is it 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983  
reasonably 
foreseeable 
that this 
material financial effect on such 
to the interest covered by Section 
have been given, it is clear that: 
decision 
will 
have 
a 
interests? 
with regards 
87103, from the facts we 
(1) 
You do not have an investment specified 
by Section 82034 worth more than $1,000 in either ARCO or 
Irving, 
(2) 
You do not have any interest worth more 
than $1,000 in real property which will be effected by this 
decision, 
(3) 
ARCO is not a source of income to you 
worth more than $250, although Irving may be~and 
(4) 
You are not a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, 
employee or manager of ARCO, 
but you do have a 
ti tIe of Associates Executive Vice President with Irving, 
although apparently this title do~ot confe~ any authority. 
From the foregoing, it appears that the material 
financial effect of the decision needs only be analyzed with 
regard to its effect on Irving as a source of income to you 
and as a business entity with which you hold a so-called 
"honoraryll title. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission has enacted 
regulations to be used to help determine if a decision will 
have a material financial effect on a source of income or a 
business entity in which the official has a management role. 
In Titl~ 2, California Administrative Code Section 18702, the 
following have,. been held to be material financial effects: 
(1) 
I f 
the effect of the decision would be to 
increase or decrease the income received by the official by 
$100 or more; 
(2 ) 
"decision and 
income; or 
I f there is a nexus between the governmental 
the purpose for which the official receives 
(3) 
If the official's source 
of 
income is 
a 
business entity, if the decision will increase or decrease 
the annualized gross revenue of that entity by the lesser of 
$100,000 or one percent, but not less than $1,000; or if it 
will increase or decrease the annual net income of the entity 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
september 30, 1983  
by the lesser of $50,000 or one half of one percent, but not 
less than $1,000; or if it will increase or decrease the 
current assets or liability of that entity by the lesser of 
$100,000 or one half of one percent, but not less than 
$1,000. 
Based upon the facts which we have been given, 
since you personally will receive no income from the approval 
or disapproval of this decision, Paragraph (1) above does not 
apply. 
Moreover, the criteria in Paragraph (3) would only be 
triggered if the gross revenue, net income or assets of 
Irving were significantly effected by the decision. 
Since 
the fee arrangement is apparently the normal consultant fee 
based upon time and materials, Irving would be compensated on 
the basis of time actually spent and not the outcome of the 
decision. 
. 
The area where we are very troubled with regard to 
Paragraph (2) and the fact that a material financial effect 
can exist is there 
~s a 
"nexus between the governmental 
decision and the purpose for which an official receives 
income." 
There are no controlling precedents on the meaning 
of this language. 
It is not inconceivable to us, however, 
that the Fair Political Practices Commission could find a 
"nexus" where between a decision to approve a CUP and a 
business relationship where a business entity has hired a 
public official to represent certain clients before govern-
mental bodies in cases involving land use decisions. 
One 
could argue that Irving's purpose in entering into a business 
relationship with a councilmember was to develop the contacts 
and influence of the councilmember with other governmental 
agencies. 
Lacking any precise definition of what the Fair 
Political Practices Commission means by the above nexus 
terminology, we are unable to say that a problem does not 
exist. 
We are further troubled by the implications of the 
common law doctrine against conflicts of interest which exist 
.entirely apart from the provisions of the Political Reform 
Act. 
Again, however, the parameters of the common law doc-
trine against conflicts of interest are extremely vague. 
As stated by the Attorney General in 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
916, 922-923: 
26w09b 
IIPrior 
to 
the 
enactment 
of 
the 
Political Reform Act of 1974, this office 
predicated decisions 
on noncontractual 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983  
conflict of interest questions 
on the 
common law rule against conflicts. 
We 
have assumed the continuing viability of 
the rule as a cumulative test despite the 
1974 initiative measure which covers both 
contractual and noncontractual matters. 
[Citation.] 
such 
doctrine 
... 
strictly requires- public 
officers 
to 
avoid placing themselves in ~ position In 
which personal interest may 
corne into 
conflict with their duty to thilPllblic.' 
[Citations.]" 
(Emphasis added.) 
Perhaps the most important word in that doctrine is 
"may,1/ which is used to indicate that the mere possibility 
that there might be a conflict between the official's duties 
to the public and to his private interests must be avoided. 
In the' situation at hand, the relationship between yourself 
and Irving which is continuous and which acts to both of your 
benefits, 
the fact .you 
dO 
work occasionally on service 
stations of the type which is in question, and the fact that 
you initially abstained from participating until sucp time as 
a tie vote was indicated, suggest that a vote by you on the 
item in question could corne within that common law prohibi-
tion, and be deemed to be malfeasance in office. 
Unfortunately, in the area of conflict of interest, 
the relevant statutes and regulations are in some instances 
vague 
and there are been some conflicting precedents in 
interpreting them. 
Although we normally strive to give clear 
advice and we are not convinced that a conflict does exist, 
in this. instance, there are enough factors involved that we 
are unable to .. say without hesitation that no conflict does 
exist. 
It would appear that the business relationships and 
understandings 
between you 
and Mr. Irving are oral and, 
therefore, possibly, subject to differing interpretations. 
It would be our suggestion, especially if it is possible 
that Mr. Irving will be representing other business clients 
.before the City Council, that the relationship between you 
and Mr. Irving be clarified or that possibly an opinion be 
26w09b 


Mr. Cecil Green 
September 30, 1983  
requested from the Fair Political Practices Commission which 
would deal with this subject. 
I look forward to discussing this matter with you 
further.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether City Councilman Cecil Green has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act that would prevent him from participating in a City Council vote on an ARCO appeal involving a conditional use permit, given that Green has a business arrangement with Neal Irving and Associates (which is representing ARCO) where they refer business to each other and share income from jobs.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Mr. Green does not have a conflict of interest in the ARCO appeal decision because Irving is not being paid on a contingent basis (so the outcome will not affect Irving's compensation), and there is no nexus between the work Green performs for Irving and the specific ARCO matter before the Council. However, the opinion cautions that Green and his counsel should carefully review all circumstances, as other potential financial effects on Irving could exist.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is a complete FPPC response letter dated December 12, 1983. Includes both the FPPC response and the incoming request letter dated October 12, 1983. The response contains detailed legal analysis of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103. Confidence reduced slightly due to some truncation in the incoming request section at bottom of document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You requested advice from this office on behalf of Cecil Green, a member of the Norwalk City Council, concerning his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. Mr. Green has a business arrangement with Mr. Neal Irving of Neal Irving and Associates, Environmental Consultants. According to your letter of September 20, 1983, to Mr. Green, Green and Irving "refer business to each other and occasionally represent each other in exchange for a percentage of the income from those particular jobs." Green has the title of Associate Executive Vice-President in Neal Irving and Associates, but he has no management authority or any investment in the company. An appeal is pending before the Norwalk City Council of a Planning Commission denial of a conditional use permit and precise development plan involving a gasoline service station. The appellant, ARCO, is represented by Neal Irving and Associates in this proceeding.

CONCLUSION: In summary, based on the facts presented, Mr. Green does not have a conflict of interest in the decision before the City Council involving the ARCO appeal. However, we give this advice with the caveat that both you and Mr. Green, who are more-familiar with all of the circumstances, should analyze the situation in light of our analysis.

When the ARCO appeal first carne before the City Council on September 12, 1983, Mr. Green stated that he would abstain from participation in the public hearing and abstain from voting. You have also indicated to me that you would like
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a request regarding whether Norwalk City Councilman Cecil Green has a conflict of interest in voting on an ARCO conditional use permit appeal, given his business referral arrangement with Neal Irving and Associates (the firm representing ARCO). The FPPC concludes no conflict exists under the Political Reform Act based on the specific facts presented, though it recommends careful analysis of all circumstances.
